death.instructions: Narrate the character returning from death in a dramatic fashion. They should retain their memories and most of their possessions, though perhaps something was lost or changed in the process. This resurrection should feel significant and have some cost or consequence, but allow the character to continue their adventure.

health.name: health
health.usageInstructions:
### Health Changes
- Small injuries: -1 to 15% player health
- Medium injuries: -5 to 25% player health
- Massive injuries: -15 to 50% of player health
- Normal Healing potions +30 - +40
- Bandages + 20
- Waking from unconsciousness: return to between half health and full health, depending on how well your recovery was.
- Being revived from death: return to full health.
### Don't Deduct Health When
- The story mentions treated or untreated wounds
- Story mentions pain from a wound
- When unconscious or carried
- An environment is generally dangerous, but does not explicitly damage the character
- A character is only looking around. Look around is a safe action.

acrobatics.name: acrobatics
acrobatics.description: Improves the player's agility and balance, enabling them to perform acrobatic maneuvers.

athletics.name: athletics
athletics.description: Improves overall physical performance, enhancing running, jumping, climbing, and other physical feats.

intimidation.name: intimidation
intimidation.description: Allows the player to use fear as a tool, potentially allowing them to scare off enemies or influence confrontations.

investigation.name: investigation
investigation.description: Enables careful examination and analysis of surroundings, helping the player uncover clues and secrets.

performance.name: performance
performance.description: Enables the player to entertain others through acts such as dancing, singing, or acting.

persuasion.name: persuasion
persuasion.description: Enhances the player's ability to influence others through dialogue.

survival.name: survival
survival.description: Improves the player's ability to endure harsh environments, find food and water, and build shelters.

Random.name: Random
Random.description: Fortune favors the bold. Begin your tale where chance decrees.
Random.storyStart: Generate a story start appropriate to the player's chosen traits. Draw on world lore to create a specific situation in a concrete location. The opening should present clear immediate circumstances while leaving many possible paths forward. Incorporate aspects of the character's background without forcing them into a predetermined narrative.

tips: There are no rules about what you type. Describe what you want to do in your own words - be as creative or detailed as you want.
Have a question about the world or the game? Use the 'Ask the Narrator' command!
Can't remember the name of a character or place? Try writing '@' followed by first few letters.
Stuck with no idea what to do next? Use the 'Ask the Narrator' command to ask for suggestions.
Worried about convincing someone? Try taking the conversation one line at a time.
Be ambitious and creative: Heroes rewards those who dream big!
How you phrase things matters: the game rewards you for stylish, clever actions.
Coordinate with party members in combat for powerful combined effects.
The world is your oyster: find and claim a place to call home.
Family is worth fighting for. Having a family in the game can lead to great stories.
You can attack multiple times per turn, and so can your allies.
Tell people your hopes and goals: you never know who may share your dreams.
Death is not always the end…
If there's something you want to do, go out and give it a try!
Try describing your actions in detail. They may not always work out the way you expect, but it will make things more interesting!
If you can't beat 'em, persuade 'em. Try convincing an enemy to come over to your side - or go over to theirs!
You can solve a problem any way you can think of: fight, bribe, trick, steal, befriend. The choice is yours.
You may spend a long time with your party members. Choose them wisely!
You can try to dodge, block and attack all in the same turn.
You can use items and take other actions in the same turn.
Skills are most likely to succeed when you use them as intended: in their natural domain.
Don't forget to stock up on items. Shops often have very powerful equipment for sale.
You can level up by taking almost any action, so feel free to play the game whatever way you want.
Don't forget to check in on your party members! Ask them how they're doing - and tell them how you feel!
Sometimes you can insta-kill enemies if you get creative with your inputs.
Party members can learn new skills and abilities as you progress through the story together.
Environmental conditions can add modifiers to your skill checks. Use the world to your advantage!
Tired of action and fighting? Look for a tavern or other gathering spot to socialize and start new relationships.
To prepare for a fight, try giving your party members orders to carry out in combat.
Social skills are very powerful. Persuasion can save a lot of lives.
Don't think you can win a fight? Try making a deal instead.
You don't have to do it all yourself, you know: hire people to do jobs for you!
Tired of traveling? Try learning how to fly or teleport or look for other forms of travel in the world.
There's nothing you can't do if you put your mind to it, from starting a revolution to founding your own country!
Turn order is determined by level and a random initiative value. Higher level characters tend to act first.
Characters without targets (like those that are buffing or healing) act before characters who are targeting enemies.
Multiple defensive effects stack multiplicatively. Layer your defenses!
Damage reduction scales with level difference, but has diminishing returns.
Different enemies can have different resistances, vulnerabilities, immunities or weaknesses, which will greatly affect the damage they take.
Try different damage types to discover enemies' weaknesse!
Healing actions use the same success level system as attacks - critical successes will heal for more.
Your equipment's damage bonus increases overall damage by 10% per point.
Equipped items with an armor stat reduce incoming damage linearly: 1000 armor grants 90% damage reduction.
Want a quest? Try asking an NPC about their problems!
Most places and characters you meet have secrets to uncover: get exploring!
Your Journal contains a wealth of information about the world's people, places and ideas.
Want to learn more about the world? Try reading books or watching television or other media!
Looking for cool gear? Don't forget to loot dead enemies and search areas where powerful items might be found!
Need some help fighting? Recruit NPCs to join your party! Anyone you meet can become an ally—if you can convince them to take your side.
Can't find the skill you want? Try asking NPCs about any new skill you can imagine. They just might be able to teach it to you.
You can unlock custom abilities when your character does new things in the game.
Need ideas about how to start your story off? Try feeding some basic concepts to the Background Generator and editing what you get back.
Don't forget to explore. Open up the map and try going to a new location.
Abilities give you bonuses to success and allow you to perform the actions they describe without ever needing any items.
The memory system remembers. Reference past events, people you've met, and places you've been - even from many turns ago!
Locations, NPCs, and quests generate dynamically based on your actions. Every playthrough creates unique content!
Don't quit after failure! Mistakes, setbacks and even death can lead to memorable stories.
Every action teaches you something. You gain XP from attempting things, not just succeeding at them.
If you don't know what to do, press the submit button to continue without an action.

authorSeeds.George R.R. Martin:
You show characters through family duties and what they hunger for
You focus on how what they want clashes with what they must do
You include specific details about their bad habits and pleasures
You create characters who act one way in public but want something else in private
You build characters around how they handle power and betrayal
authorSeeds.Joe Abercrombie:
You show characters through their dark view of life and painful past
You focus on scars, limps, and old wounds
You create characters who joke about terrible things
You build characters who make excuses for doing bad things
You reveal character through how they fight or survive
authorSeeds.Brandon Sanderson:
You show characters through how they practice their skills and powers
You focus on the rules they carefully follow
You create characters with unique ways of solving problems
You build characters who find clever new uses for their skills
You reveal character through their personal codes and habits
authorSeeds.N.K. Jemisin:
You show characters through their fight against or place within power systems
You focus on how they've survived unfair treatment
You include specific cultural traditions they've kept or changed
You create characters struggling to stay true to themselves
You reveal character through how they resist or go along with the system
authorSeeds.Mark Lawrence:
You show characters through their amazing skills and moral gray areas
You focus on how past pain has made them dangerous
You include cold planning hidden behind charm
You create characters who are great at working situations to their advantage
You reveal character through their expert understanding of violence
authorSeeds.Robin Hobb:
You show characters through deep emotional hurts and how they grow
You focus on how their work has shaped who they think they are
You include small habits that show old hurts or fears
You create characters who stay proud despite inner struggles
You build characters shaped by everyday relationships with regular people
authorSeeds.Steven Erikson:
You show characters through how they see their place in the world
You focus on how living a long time has changed how they think
You include mysterious hints about past events that changed them
You create characters dealing with knowing too much
You build characters who make dark jokes about fate
authorSeeds.Guy Gavriel Kay:
You show characters through how art and duty mix in their lives
You focus on how their people's history shapes what they do
You include moments where they notice beauty in everyday things
You create characters who carry on traditions with grace
You build characters who find beauty in ordinary life
authorSeeds.Scott Lynch:
You show characters through their backup plans for their backup plans
You focus on their quick thinking and calculated risks
You include odd knowledge that hints at a colorful past
You create characters who keep several lies going at once
You build characters known for creative cursing and sharp comebacks
authorSeeds.Adrian Tchaikovsky:
You show characters through unusual views on normal situations
You focus on how special knowledge colors how they see everything
You include surprising skills that turn out to matter
You create characters who adapt their abilities to weird situations
You build characters who solve problems in unusual ways
authorSeeds.Terry Pratchett:
You show characters through their down-to-earth wisdom about life
You focus on how they stay sensible in crazy situations
You create characters who use jokes to point out deeper truths
You build characters known for being stubbornly decent or cheerfully cynical
You reveal character through creative solutions to everyday problems
authorSeeds.Lois McMaster Bujold:
You show characters through family duties and personal honor
You focus on how work duties clash with personal loyalties
You create characters balancing many social responsibilities
You build characters shaped by family traditions and personal beliefs
You reveal character through how they handle competing duties
authorSeeds.China Miéville:
You show characters through their connection to art or big ideas
You focus on how their beliefs change how they see everything
You create characters who don't fit normal categories
You build characters driven by revolutionary or boundary-breaking urges
You reveal character through how they deal with society's structures
authorSeeds.Katherine Addison:
You show characters through how they navigate social rules
You focus on how they keep their dignity within strict hierarchies
You create characters who use politeness as a weapon
You build characters shaped by their official duties
You reveal character through their mastery of proper manners
authorSeeds.Leigh Bardugo:
You show characters through their big plans and personal grudges
You focus on how past poverty drives what they want now
You create characters who turn their weaknesses into strengths
You build characters driven by desperate need to prove themselves
You reveal character through clever ways they gain power or money
authorSeeds.Ada Palmer:
You show characters through how they organize their thinking
You focus on how their expertise colors everything they see
You create characters who approach personal matters like academics
You build characters who sort the world in unique ways
You reveal character through specialized language and thinking habits
authorSeeds.Ursula K. Le Guin:
You show characters through their relationship with learning and teaching
You focus on how they balance guiding others while letting them grow
You create characters who question traditional knowledge
You build characters shaped by their duty to help others grow
You reveal character through how they pass on wisdom
authorSeeds.Becky Chambers:
You show characters through their curiosity about new things
You focus on how they connect across differences
You create characters who explore with heart as well as mind
You build characters shaped by wonder at new experiences
You reveal character through how they share what they discover
authorSeeds.Richard K. Morgan:
You show characters through their handling of power and control
You focus on how being capable affects moral boundaries
You create characters with strict professional standards
You build characters who mix personal and professional ethics
You reveal character through their skilled approach to conflict

characterArchetypes.Survivor:
Drives:
- staying alive
- getting what they need
- avoiding danger

Traits:
- clever
- always alert
- quick to change plans

Morality: does what works

Relationships:
- makes useful friends
- has backup plans
- tests limits

Relationships:

- makes excuses

generateStory.Victory and Downtime:
## Pacing
- Let the current scene's purpose set the pace: action moves quickly, focused conversations stay with the speakers, and quiet moments can breathe
- Do not introduce a new threat, rival, betrayal, ticking clock, or complication just to keep tension high
- If the party has clearly won, escaped, rested, celebrated, or resolved the immediate pressure, let the aftermath land before adding new pressure
- During downtime, rest, recovery, travel, shopping, or celebration, focus on the activity the players chose unless an existing threat directly intrudes
- Escalate only from established causes: player actions, NPC motives, visible danger, active conflicts, or consequences already in motion
- Stay with the current scene unless the prompt explicitly asks for a cutaway
- End the beat once the player action and immediate reactions are resolved, without adding a final mood summary or teaser
generateStory.Character Behavior:
## Character Behavior
- Characters act from coherent, consistent motivations shaped by personality, role, relationships, goals, and what they know
- Show personality through dialogue, choices, and actions rather than narrator explanation
- Give different characters distinct voices: vocabulary, rhythm, formality, confidence, humor, and emotional restraint should vary by character
- Characters can be kind, funny, evasive, mistaken, selfish, afraid, loyal, hostile, or conflicted as fits the situation
- Let characters show mixed motives and emotions rather than a single simple mood
- NPCs know only what they could plausibly know; information spreads through witnesses, rumors, records, magic, messages, or direct experience
- NPCs should not deliver lectures, technical explanations, lore summaries, or system explanations unless it fits their role and the moment
- Dialogue should sound natural and purposeful; avoid stiff phrasing, repeated reminders, and unnecessary restatement
- Do not summarize, paraphrase, or indirectly report spoken dialogue; write the spoken words as character dialogue lines
generateStory.Style Principles:
## Style Principles
- Use simple, direct, concrete language in third person present tense
- Prefer strong verbs and specific nouns over adjective-heavy or adverb-heavy description
- Favor action, consequence, and dialogue over atmosphere or low-value description
- When characters are engaged with each other, keep narrator interruptions sparse and relevant
- Only mention background activity when it affects the immediate action, choice, or character perception
- Describe what characters say and do; avoid explaining how skillful, dramatic, important, or meaningful the action is
- Name precise new visible details when they matter, but do not re-describe established people, places, objects, or moods
- Keep description tied to new elements the player characters can perceive, inspect, or immediately act on
- Treat hidden information and setting details as behavior guidance, not narrator or character knowledge; reveal secrets only through play, observation, investigation, or conversation
- Avoid stock physical reactions and vague emotional shorthand
- Vary sentence openings, sentence length, and word choice across paragraphs
- Avoid vague filler such as generic tension, charged silence, unexplained emotional shifts, or repeated atmospheric beats
- Keep language fresh and functional; if a phrase, image, or construction appeared recently, choose a different one

generateInitialStart.Opening Structure:
## Opening Structure
- First beat: NARRATOR sets the scene with brief description of place, who the PCs are, and what they're doing
- Where appropriate, briefly show the time of year, season and weather
- USE VISUAL AND AUDITORY DESCRIPTION ONLY UNLESS ABSOLUTELY NECESSARY: NO SMELLS PLEASE
- Then begin an active, dialog-driven scene with clear interaction opportunities, within the content type specified in the starting situation (slice of life, combat, social interaction, etc.)
- However as an exception, if the starting situation indicates the character is alone, respect the author's intent to have the character remain solitary.
- Weave in additional narration beats as needed
- When multiple PCs present, show them all in the opening scene
- Avoid portraying PC relationships except as stated in the starting situation
- Once you've set up the scene, stop narrating and let the player take over
generateInitialStart.Style Principles:
## Style Principles
- Be specific, concise and concrete
- Use simple, direct, literal language
- Favor strong verbs and nouns over adjectives and adverbs
- Vary sentence length but prefer shorter sentences
- Use dialog to reveal character and provide story context
- Prefer dialogue to description
- Be grounded yet original, avoiding clichés
- Avoid conceptual or worldbuilding weirdness, but feel free to make characters quirky where appropriate

generateCharacterBackground.prompt:
# Character Profile Generator
Write a character profile in two parts: background and appearance.

Use the World Foundation as the main setting context. If World Lore is provided, weave in at most one element that genuinely helps the character feel rooted in the world rather than decorated by it.

Use the Character Foundation as your main guide to who this person is. Trait descriptions are the strongest clue to role, temperament, social position, and beginner-level abilities. Trait quirks, listed skills, and notable attributes should sharpen that impression, not override it.

If Creative Direction is provided, treat it as player-authored intent. Preserve its core ideas unless they directly conflict with the world.

For background, write the history and tension that make this person feel alive and specific. Focus on where they come from, what shaped them, what they are becoming, and what they want or lack.

For appearance, describe the physical and social impression they make. Focus on concrete, observable details and the effect of their presence on others, not vague interpretation.

Keep the profile vivid, specific, and grounded. Avoid generic phrasing, stock archetypes, and filler. Let characters be ambitious, compromised, selfish, romantic, frightened, hungry, or morally difficult if the world supports it.

Match the requested style, tone, and length implied by the world's instructions. Do not add headers or labels inside the field contents.
